chunk_summary: Defines moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in Indian clinics using WHO criteria, emphasizing diagnostic accuracy, misclassification pitfalls, and outpatient management for uncomplicated cases.
learning_objectives: Understand WHO criteria for diagnosing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children aged 6–59 months.
Identify key diagnostic metrics like MUAC and weight-for-height z-scores for MAM in Indian clinics.
Learn to differentiate MAM from underweight and wasting with complications.
Apply practical bedside screening methods for MAM during sick-child visits.
key_facts: WHO defines MAM as weight-for-height/length z-scores between -2 and -3 and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema.
Indian clinics use a safety-first definition: moderate wasting by either metric, clinically stable, with appetite, and no acute danger signs.
Underweight (low weight-for-age) should not be equated with MAM, as it may indicate chronic stunting.
Persistent pitting oedema is checked by pressing both feet for several seconds.
Indian manuals often describe MAM as 70–80% of median weight-for-height or MUAC 115–125 mm, managed as outpatient cases.
reference_quote: WHO defines moderate wasting as weight-for-height/length z-scores between -2 and -3 and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema. (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Outpatient management of uncomplicated MAM cases
